coredata.dc:title: Lithium-ion battery remaining useful life prediction for portable medical device safety: a VMD-RVM based approach
coredata.prism:publicationName: Applications in Energy and Combustion Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Tiantong
Jin, Hongyang
Zhang, Chenyu
Wang, Huaibin
coredata.dc:description: Medical monitors typically come equipped with lithium-ion batteries as a source of energy supply. With the popularity of monitors, battery management has become an increasingly important indicator for evaluating the effectiveness of monitor usage. The Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of a lithium battery is an important parameter that characterizes the functionality of a battery management system. Accurate prediction of the RUL of a lithium battery can ensure the smooth operation of the battery equipment and guarantee the utilization efficiency of the monitor. In response to the problem of low estimation accuracy caused by complex estimation models in current lithium battery RUL prediction, a lithium battery RUL prediction method using Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD) and Relevance Vector Machine (RVM) is proposed by selecting the time when the discharge phase voltage drops to the cut-off voltage as the health indicator. Firstly, the time when the voltage drops to the cut-off voltage during the discharge stage is used as a health indicator, and Spearman correlation analysis is used to determine that the voltage drop time has a strong correlation with the battery's capacity data. Secondly, due to the phenomenon of capacity regeneration in battery capacity, VMD is used to decompose the voltage drop time signal, and RVM is used for training. Finally, the voltage drop time data is input into the VMD-RVM model to obtain the predicted RUL of the battery. Verified using NASA's publicly available dataset, the experimental results show that the VMD-RVM prediction method used in this paper has lower MAE and RMSE compared to EEMD-RVM and EMD-RVM, indicating that the proposed method has higher accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Medical devices
Variational mode decomposition
Relevance vector machine
Battery safety
Remaining useful life

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Lithium-ion battery RUL prediction based on VMD-RVM
Lithium-ion battery RUL prediction model based on VMD-RVM
Selection of the number of modal components in VMD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Potential problems caused by the lack of RUL prediction for lithium-ion batteries in medical devices.
Capacity degradation curves of NASA lithium-ion batteries.
Variation of discharge voltage with time for Battery B0005.
VMD decomposition diagram of capacity data for battery B0005.
Description of lithium-ion battery test dataset.

row.entry: Charging was performed in 1.5 A CC mode until the battery voltage reached 4.2 V
Discharging was performed at a constant current of 2 A until the voltages of Batteries 5, 6, 7, and 18 dropped to 2.7 V, 2.5 V, 2.2 V, and 2.5 V, respectively
Measured by Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) with a frequency scan from 0.1 Hz to 5 kHz
Charging was continued in CV mode until the charging current dropped to 20 mA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Correlation analysis between HI and battery capacity.
Central frequencies corresponding to different K values for the health indicator of the B0005 battery.
Spearman's correlation coefficients between different IMFs and the original data.
The center frequencies of IMFs corresponding to different K values.
Spearman's correlation coefficients between different IMFs and the original data.
Comparison of evaluation metrics of different models.

head.ce:title: Lithium-ion battery remaining useful life prediction for portable medical device safety: a VMD-RVM based approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Chinese PLA General Hospital, Beijing, 100853, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Biomedical Engineering, Chinese PLA General Hospital, Beijing, 100853, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China People’s Police University, LangFang, 065000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bChina People’s Police University, LangFang, 065000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Medical monitors typically come equipped with lithium-ion batteries as a source of energy supply. With the popularity of monitors, battery management has become an increasingly important indicator for evaluating the effectiveness of monitor usage. The Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of a lithium battery is an important parameter that characterizes the functionality of a battery management system. Accurate prediction of the RUL of a lithium battery can ensure the smooth operation of the battery equipment and guarantee the utilization efficiency of the monitor. In response to the problem of low estimation accuracy caused by complex estimation models in current lithium battery RUL prediction, a lithium battery RUL prediction method using Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD) and Relevance Vector Machine (RVM) is proposed by selecting the time when the discharge phase voltage drops to the cut-off voltage as the health indicator. Firstly, the time when the voltage drops to the cut-off voltage during the discharge stage is used as a health indicator, and Spearman correlation analysis is used to determine that the voltage drop time has a strong correlation with the battery's capacity data. Secondly, due to the phenomenon of capacity regeneration in battery capacity, VMD is used to decompose the voltage drop time signal, and RVM is used for training. Finally, the voltage drop time data is input into the VMD-RVM model to obtain the predicted RUL of the battery. Verified using NASA's publicly available dataset, the experimental results show that the VMD-RVM prediction method used in this paper has lower MAE and RMSE compared to EEMD-RVM and EMD-RVM, indicating that the proposed method has higher accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries, with advantages such as high energy density [1–3] and long service life [4], can provide continuous power support for portable medical devices (e.g., ventilators, blood glucose meters, heart rate monitors). The stability of medical devices is directly related to medical quality and patient health safety [5], so their safety and reliability must be considered during application. However, complex physical and chemical reactions occurring inside the battery during use lead to increased internal resistance and decreased capacity, resulting in gradual performance degradation and eventual failure [6]. Among these failures, thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries is a critical failure mode, characterized by an uncontrolled increase in temperature [7]. Therefore, the safety management of lithium-ion batteries is of great importance [8]. By predicting the RUL of lithium-ion batteries, users can be prompted to repair or replace the batteries in a timely manner, effectively preventing thermal failures of lithium-ion batteries and ensuring the long-term stable operation of portable medical devices. Fig. 1 shows the potential problems caused by the lack of RUL prediction for lithium-ion batteries in medical devices.

The RUL of lithium-ion batteries refers to the number of charge-discharge cycles from the current state to the end of the battery’s life [9]. The mainstream methods for predicting the RUL of lithium-ion batteries at home and abroad are mainly divided into two categories: model-based methods and data-driven methods [10,11].

Model-based methods rely on the physical and empirical laws of battery degradation behavior for modeling [12], and can be mainly classified into electrochemical models, equivalent circuit models, and empirical models [13]. Electrochemical models analyze the internal aging state of batteries based on the principle of battery charge and discharge. However, the parameters of electrochemical models are difficult to measure, and their dynamic accuracy is poor, making them difficult to apply in practical scenarios [14,15]. Equivalent circuit models construct equivalent circuits using basic circuit components according to the electrical characteristics of batteries, simulate the dynamic characteristics of batteries through circuit components, and reflect the degree of aging through parameter changes [16]. However, the physicochemical properties of batteries change over time, so this method is easily interfered by environmental or noise factors, making it difficult to track the dynamic characteristics of loads, and has poor dynamic accuracy, robustness, and adaptability [17]. Empirical models continuously fit the battery degradation trajectory using different functions by analyzing a large amount of experimental data and select the function with the best effect as the empirical model. Although these models have advantages in interpretability, they usually require in-depth knowledge of battery physics and electrochemistry, and are limited by the complexity of equivalent circuit structures and internal mechanisms [18]. Therefore, they are often unable to fully characterize the complex dynamic and static behaviors of lithium-ion batteries under different operating conditions [19].

Data-driven methods do not rely on the internal chemical reaction mechanism of batteries; instead, they mine and analyze historical data (e.g., voltage, current, temperature, cycle count) to obtain relevant feature data, and use machine learning algorithms to predict the variation law of lithium-ion battery RUL [20]. Data-driven RUL prediction methods mainly consist of three parts: data extraction, RUL prediction model establishment, and RUL prediction. Muhammad Umair Ali [21] et al. extracted HIs such as voltage and temperature from partial discharge data and input them into the Support Vector Machine (SVM). The experimental results showed that the SVM classification and regression models trained using partial discharge data features could accurately predict RUL with low storage pressure on BMS; Ahwiadi M [22] et al. proposed an enhanced particle filter method, which introduced an evolutionary fuzzy predictor into the improved Particle Filter (PF) structure to solve the problem of insufficient new battery measurement data during prediction; the experimental results verified the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm; Wang [23] et al. proposed an SVM-based RUL prediction method for lithium-ion batteries based on historical degradation data of lithium-ion batteries, and introduced the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm to optimize the kernel parameters of Support Vector Regression (SVR); the results showed that this method had high prediction accuracy and stability in lithium-ion battery RUL prediction; Toughzaoui [24] proposed a K-means clustering data classification algorithm, which not only simplified the feature mining process of LSTM and CNN but also improved the accuracy of RUL estimation; Yang [25] et al. proposed a hybrid model based on Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition (EEMD) and SVR optimized by the Gray Wolf Optimization (GWO) algorithm, which not only solved the problem of capacity regeneration but also improved the accuracy of prediction results. Due to their good accuracy and robustness, data-driven methods are often used for lithium-ion battery RUL prediction [26].

Lithium-ion batteries themselves are highly complex and dynamic electrochemical systems [27]. At present, the prediction of lithium-ion battery RUL requires continuous improvement in accuracy [28]. Starting from the safety of battery systems, this paper proposes a lithium-ion battery RUL prediction method based on VMD and RVM. First, the time for the discharge voltage to drop to a specific value (voltage drop time) is selected as an indirect HI for predicting battery capacity, and Spearman correlation analysis is used to prove the strong correlation between the voltage drop time and battery capacity. Second, to address the capacity regeneration phenomenon of batteries, VMD is used to filter the voltage drop time. Finally, the RVM method is used to train a lithium-ion battery RUL prediction model, thereby realizing the prediction of lithium-ion battery life and further improving the safety of lithium-ion battery use.

Portable medical devices are constrained by "lightweight" requirements, while the RVM based on the Bayesian framework can integrate prior information to enhance the robustness of inference under small sample sizes. Coupled with lightweight signal processing via VMD to adapt to small sample noise and RVM's sparse computation to suit low-power hardware, it ultimately achieves "sufficient and reliable" predictive performance under scenario constraints.

The main contributions of this paper are as follows:
(1)
The time for the battery voltage to drop to the cut-off voltage during the discharge phase is selected as the HI, and VMD is used to filter the selected indicator to remove noise caused by battery capacity regeneration, reducing the impact of noise on battery RUL prediction accuracy.
(2)
A VMD-RVM model is constructed by combining the RVM algorithm. By comparing the two kernel function combinations of EMD-RVM and EEMD-RVM, it is found that the combination effect based on VMD-RVM is better than the other combinations.
(3)
Experimental verification is conducted using the public lithium-ion battery dataset provided by the NASA Prognostics Center of Excellence, proving that the method proposed in this paper has higher prediction accuracy.

The lithium-ion battery test data used in this study is from the public lithium-ion battery aging experiment dataset of the NASA Prognostics Center of Excellence [29]. The data was collected from 18,650-type batteries with a rated capacity of 2000 mAh, subjected to repeated charge-discharge cycles at 24 °C. The description of the lithium-ion battery test dataset is shown in Table 1 .

The Health Indicator (HI) refers to a basic indicator that can reflect the health status of a battery, and is divided into indirect HI and direct HI. The reasonable selection of HI largely determines the prediction accuracy of the model. Battery capacity is an important indicator for evaluating the battery health state and predicting battery RUL. Fig. 2 shows the capacity degradation curves of Batteries B0005, B0006, and B0007.

Due to the existence of capacity regeneration in most lithium-ion batteries [30], directly using battery capacity to predict battery RUL makes it difficult to accurately represent the degradation trend of battery capacity. Therefore, many studies first perform signal decomposition on battery capacity data and then predict battery RUL to improve accuracy. Based on the aging characteristics of batteries, this paper selects an indirect HI that can characterize the battery aging trend from the voltage data of battery datasets, and takes Battery B0005 as an example to explain the extraction of the HI.

Taking Battery B0005 as an example, Fig. 3 shows the variation of the battery’s discharge voltage with time during the 1st, 30th, 60th, 90th, 120th, and 150th cycles. As can be seen from Fig. 3, at the initial stage of the discharge phase, the voltage first drops sharply, followed by a slow voltage drop (this stage is usually called the Discharge Plateau [31]), and then the battery continues to discharge until the voltage reaches the cut-off voltage, marking the end of the entire discharge phase. Meanwhile, as the number of cycles increases, the time for the battery to discharge to the cut-off voltage gradually shortens; thus, this time can be used as the HI.

Based on the discharge voltage curve of the lithium-ion battery data, an HI was selected. To further illustrate the ability of the selected indirect HI to represent battery capacity data, Spearman correlation analysis was used to evaluate the correlation between the HI and battery capacity data. The Spearman correlation coefficient is shown in Eq. (1):
(1)
r s
=
ρ
R
( x )
, R
( y )
=
cov ( R
( x )
, R
( y )

R
( x )

σ
R
( y )
where,
cov ( R ( x ) , R ( y ) )
is the covariance of the rank variables, and
σ
R ( x )
·
σ
R ( y )
are the standard deviations of the rank variables. The Spearman correlation analysis between the selected indirect HI and battery capacity data is shown in Table 2 .

As can be seen from Table 2, the three selected HIs all have a strong negative correlation with battery capacity, indicating that they contain battery degradation information. Although the data collection process is affected by noise, it is feasible to select the voltage drop time as the HI for the input of the VMD-RVM model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithium-ion battery RUL prediction based on VMD-RVM

ce:section.ce:para:
Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD) is a signal processing technology used to decompose complex signals into several sub-signals (modes) with different frequency ranges. Its basic idea is to minimize the bandwidth of each modal signal through optimization, enabling the decomposition of complex signals into a set of sub-signals with specific frequencies and bandwidths. VMD overcomes certain shortcomings of traditional decomposition methods (e.g., Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD)) and has higher robustness and decomposition accuracy. The corresponding constrained variational model is shown in Eq. (2):
(2)

u k
}

( t )

K

= f

To solve this equation, a quadratic penalty term and Lagrange multiplier are introduced to convert it into an unconstrained problem. The obtained unconstrained Lagrangian expression is shown in Eq. (3):
(3)

The Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) is used to iteratively solve the saddle point problem of the augmented Lagrangian function. The modal functions and their corresponding center frequencies are updated iteratively until the stopping condition is met. The iterative update formulas for the modal functions and center frequencies are shown in Eq. (4):
(4)

Relevance Vector Machine (RVM) is a supervised learning algorithm based on Bayesian principles. It selects the optimal model parameters by maximizing the marginal likelihood, and obtains relevance vectors and weights. Let
{ x }

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithium-ion battery RUL prediction model based on VMD-RVM
ce:section.ce:para: This paper proposes a method that uses VMD for data decomposition to eliminate data non-stationarity, and then uses RVM to predict the RUL of lithium-ion batteries. The flowchart of the steps is shown in Fig. 4 :

Step 1: Acquire lithium-ion battery capacity degradation data, extract the time for the voltage to drop to the cut-off voltage during the discharge phase as the HI for predicting lithium-ion battery RUL, and calculate the correlation using Spearman correlation analysis.

Step 2: Perform VMD decomposition on the voltage drop time data to reduce the interference of data non-stationarity (caused by factors such as battery capacity regeneration during charge-discharge cycles) on prediction accuracy.
Step 3: Input the processed training set data into the VMD-RVM model for training.

Step 4: Input the test set into the RVM model for RUL prediction; use data from cycles 1 to T as the training dataset, and data from cycles T + 1 to the end as the validation dataset to verify the model accuracy. The flowchart of the model in this paper is shown below:

To quantitatively evaluate the prediction performance of the proposed algorithm, three commonly used metrics—Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE)—were used to measure the prediction accuracy of the method.

The calculation formulas are as follows:
(12)
M A E =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
|
y ′
( i )
− y
( i )
|
×
100 %
(13)
R M S E =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
|
y ′
( i )
− y
( i )
|
2
×
100 %

Where
y ′
( i )
is the predicted value of lithium-ion battery RUL,
y ( i )
is the actual value of lithium-ion battery RUL, N is the number of prediction cycles. Ideally, lower values of RMSE and MAE indicate smaller prediction errors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of the number of modal components in VMD
ce:section.ce:para:
VMD can adaptively decompose the original signal into K intrinsic mode functions (IMFs), each corresponding to a central frequency, sorted from high to low (IMF1 is the high-frequency component, and
I M
F K
is the low-frequency component). A reasonable K is the low-frequency component). A reasonable K should cover the main frequency components of the signal while ensuring that the central frequencies of the IMFs are uniformly distributed, with distinct intervals and no repetition. If K is too small, important frequency components of the signal may not be fully separated, resulting in information loss and thus affecting subsequent prediction accuracy. If K is too large, it may easily lead to mode repetition or introduce redundant noise components [27]. Therefore, the optimal number of decomposition modes can be determined by analyzing the distribution characteristics of central frequencies under different K values [28].
Taking the B0005 battery as an example, when the extracted health indicator is decomposed using VMD, the central frequencies corresponding to different values of K are shown in Table 3 .

When K = 5, the central frequencies obtained are very close to those when K = 4, indicating that the VMD algorithm results in over-decomposition of the signal. When K = 3, due to the large gap between the central frequencies of IMF3 and IMF4, the signal decomposition is insufficient. Considering both the uniformity of the central frequency distribution and the sufficiency of decomposition, the optimal number of modes K for VMD decomposition of the health indicator is determined to be 4. Meanwhile, the results of the correlation analysis between each IMF and the original health indicator are shown in Table 4 .

It can be seen that after decomposing the health factor using VMD, IMF3 has a very strong correlation with the original data. IMF3 can be used as a trend signal to input into the RVM model for training. Similarly, after decomposing the capacity data, are shown in the Table 5 below.

When K = 3, the decomposed center frequency differs very little from that when K = 2, and when K = 2, the center frequency of IMF1 and IMF2 show a significant difference. Therefore, the optimal K value is 2. When K = 2, the Spearman correlation coefficients between IMF1, IMF2, and the original data are shown in the Table 6 below.

Through correlation analysis between each IMF obtained from VMD decomposition and the original capacity data, it is clear that IMF2 exhibits the strongest correlation. This indicates that IMF2 not only effectively filters out high-frequency noise but also retains the core trend information closely related to the battery capacity degradation process. Therefore, IMF2 is selected for subsequent modeling of lithium battery remaining useful life prediction.

The public NASA lithium-ion battery dataset was used to verify the accuracy of the VMD-RVM lithium-ion battery RUL prediction model. First, three battery models (B0005, B0006, and B0007) were selected, and their battery capacity degradation data and voltage drop time data were collected. Then, VMD was used to perform signal decomposition on the health state data and voltage drop time of the three battery models. Fig. 5 shows the decomposition diagram of the capacity state data of Battery B0005.

Among them, IMF1 is high-frequency components, arranged in descending order of frequency. IMF2 is the trend component. The denoised curve is consistent with the variation trend of the original curve, indicating the effectiveness of this method. Spearman analysis was used to analyze the correlation between the battery health state and the voltage drop time during discharge. The correlation coefficients for B0005, B0006, and B0007 were −0.9997, −0.9998, and −0.9991, respectively, indicating a strong correlation between battery capacity degradation data and the voltage drop time to the cut-off voltage. Finally, the first 100 cycles of data were selected as the training data to train the RVM model for RUL prediction, and the last 68 cycles were used as the test data. MAE and RMSE were selected as the evaluation metrics.

To illustrate the superiority of the method proposed in this paper, a comparative analysis of multiple prediction simulation methods was conducted. The algorithms compared with the proposed algorithm include EEMD-RVM, EMD-RVM, and RVM. The comparison of evaluation results of different models is shown in Table 7 .

As can be seen from the data in Table 3, for Batteries B0005, B0006, and B0007, the proposed method has a smaller deviation from the true values, and the prediction results are better than those of other models. Taking Battery B0005 as an example, comparing the MAE and RMSE metrics, the VMD-RVM prediction method proposed in this paper has lower MAE and RMSE than EEMD-RVM and EMD-RVM, which proves that the proposed method has higher prediction accuracy.

The generalization capability of the VMD-RVM model was verified through experiments using the lithium-ion battery dataset provided by the Center for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering (CALCE)** at the University of Maryland, USA. This dataset exhibits distinct characteristics compared to the battery dataset from NASA, which allows for a more comprehensive and rigorous evaluation of the model’s generalization performance across different data distributions. For the experimental setup, data from Battery CS2_35 in the CALCE dataset was selected as the test object. A training-to-test split ratio of 60:40 was adopted, where 60 % of the data was used for model training and the remaining 40 % for performance validation. Experimental results indicated that the model achieved high prediction accuracy on Battery CS2_35. Specifically, the evaluation metrics yielded a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.00936 and a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 0.01325. These results confirm that the VMD-RVM model maintains stable and reliable performance when applied to datasets with different characteristics, thereby demonstrating its excellent generalization capability. Detailed prediction results are presented in Fig. 6 .

The prediction of the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of equipment and its key components is not only an important means for equipment fault diagnosis and prediction but also a prerequisite for equipment preventive maintenance. Focusing on the safety operation requirements of lithium-ion batteries in portable medical devices, this paper proposes an RUL prediction method based on Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD) and Relevance Vector Machine (RVM), aiming to reduce the safety risks caused by battery failure through high-precision life prediction. Verification based on the battery data provided by NASA shows that compared with EEMD-RVM and EMD-RVM, VMD-RVM significantly reduces the errors between the predicted values and the true values in terms of MAE and RMSE, improving the overall robustness and accuracy of the prediction method. The main conclusions of this paper are as follows:
(1)
A lithium-ion battery RUL prediction method for portable medical device safety is proposed, which can effectively solve the problems of high complexity and insufficient accuracy of traditional models.
(2)
The time for the voltage to drop to the cut-off voltage during the discharge phase has a strong negative correlation with battery capacity. The discharge voltage drop time, as an HI, can effectively characterize the battery degradation state.
(3)
The VMD-RVMbased RUL prediction method can improve the prediction accuracy. It can be directly applied to the battery management system of medical devices, reducing the risk of equipment shutdown caused by sudden failure.

However, this study does not deeply consider the physical mechanism of capacity regeneration (e.g., the impact of cycle count and temperature on the activity of electrode materials). In the future, a dynamic model of capacity regeneration will be integrated, and a multi-physics coupling model will be constructed based on the battery side reaction mechanism and embedded into the VMD-RVM framework to improve the reliability of long-term prediction. Additionally, the method will be applied to RUL prediction in complex scenarios such as multiple operating conditions, and further extended to the RUL prediction of complex equipment systems.
Tiantong Chen: Writing – review & editing. Hongyang Jin: Writing – original draft. Chenyu Zhang: Writing – original draft. Huaibin Wang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the support of the Key Research Special Project of CPPU (ZDZX202501).
